talk_title: Mapping the Molecular Landscape of Head and Neck Cancer to Inform Pan‑Tumor Targeting

speaker.name: Lillian L. Siu
speaker.affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, University of Toronto, Senior Medical Oncologist, Director of the Phase 1 Clinical Trials Program

biomarkers.name: ctDNA MRD
biomarkers.finding: Positive ctDNA after definitive therapy predicted relapse with lead time 100–245 days; MRD‑negative patients had markedly better relapse‑free survival (HR significant).

biomarkers.name: HPV DNA in plasma
biomarkers.finding: HPV‑positive ctDNA identified early relapse and stratified patients similarly to mutation‑based MRD.

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Required for enrollment on bispecific arm; all randomized patients were PD‑L1 positive.

biomarkers.name: TCR repertoire richness
biomarkers.finding: Patients with decreasing ctDNA showed increased TCR diversity; rising ctDNA correlated with reduced repertoire richness.

models.name: MERIDIAN
models.genotype_or_key_features: locally advanced HNSCC (oral cavity, oropharynx, larynx, hypopharynx), stage III‑IV, HPV‑positive or high‑risk HPV‑negative, PD‑L1 positive, no macroscopic disease

models.name: PREMERIDIAN feasibility cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈17 patients with curative‑intent surgery/chemoradiation, serial plasma sampling

clinical_data.metric: Relapse‑free survival hazard ratio (MRD‑positive vs MRD‑negative)

MOA_summary: Dr. Siu demonstrated that ultrasensitive tumor‑informed ctDNA (RADAR) and HPV‑DNA assays can detect molecular residual disease months before radiographic recurrence in head‑and‑neck cancer. Randomization of MRD‑positive patients to a PD‑1‑targeting bispecific antibody (revagostumab) resulted in durable disease‑free survival, supporting the concept of early immunotherapy interception based on real‑time MRD status.
key_findings: Feasibility study (PREMERIDIAN) showed 100% blood sample capture in 17 patients and identified ctDNA lead times of 100–245 days before clinical recurrence.

In MERIDIAN, only 5/42 evaluable patients were ctDNA‑positive at early post‑treatment time points, and most of those already had macroscopic disease, highlighting the need for radiologic confirmation of MRD.
MRD‑negative patients (≈35) had excellent relapse‑free outcomes (30/35 alive without recurrence, median follow‑up >22 months).
The single patient randomized to revagostumab remained disease‑free for >22 months, whereas the observation patient progressed, providing proof‑of‑concept for MRD‑guided immunotherapy interception.
Longitudinal ctDNA sampling (multiple post‑treatment windows) increased detection sensitivity compared with single landmark testing.
Early ctDNA kinetics (day 8) after PD‑1 blockade in advanced disease correlated with later response, and decreasing ctDNA was associated with increased peripheral TCR diversity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MRD detection sensitivity varied by assay type; tumor‑informed assays outperformed tumor‑agnostic panels.
Some ctDNA‑positive results coincided with overt radiologic disease, indicating false‑positive MRD if imaging is not integrated.
Limited assay sensitivity in head‑and‑neck cancer compared with other sites (e.g., colorectal) may restrict broader applicability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target residual disease with potent immune activation before macroscopic relapse.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Disease‑free for >22 months in MRD‑positive patient vs progression in observation.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine tumor‑associated antigen targeting with checkpoint blockade to overcome immune resistance in advanced HNSCC.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Planned study; no efficacy data yet.

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and number of ctDNA sampling points to maximize MRD detection without over‑testing?
Can newer ultrasensitive assays increase the proportion of true MRD‑positive patients eligible for interception?
How generalizable are head‑and‑neck MRD kinetics and bispecific immunotherapy benefits to other tumor types?

cited_papers: Signatera assay validation studies (clinical cancer research)
Siu et al. 2022 Cancer Cell perspective on adaptive trial design
